blurb: One of the largest trees on the Front Range — a fast, broad-crowned native cottonwood of streambanks and bottomlands; triangular fluttering leaves, brilliant clear-yellow fall, and massive winter architecture. Females release the famous early-summer cotton.
size: ~60–100 ft tall × 35–60 ft wide (a true shade-giant)
water: High — moist bottomland; not for dry zones, thirsty + brittle
spread: Single broad-crowned trunk; suckers and seeds into stands near water
seasons: Yellow-green catkins on bare twigs early spring; cottony seed in early summer; brilliant clear-yellow fall; deeply furrowed gray bark and bold bare architecture in winter
wildlife: Keystone riparian tree — cavity-nest and roost sites, a larval host for mourning cloak, viceroy and tiger swallowtail, and cover/browse for deer, beaver and birds
deer: Seedlings and young trees browsed by deer, rabbits and beaver — not deer-proof
toxic: No serious toxicity; brittle wood drops limbs and the greedy roots heave pavement, lift sewer lines and crowd foundations

care.hardiness: Bulletproof here — rated USDA zones 3a–9b, so it sails through Boulder's 5b–6a winters with no protection.[1]
care.planting: Plant dormant in spring or early fall while the tree can knit roots before heat or hard frost; it transplants easily and grows fast. Site it deliberately at least 30–50 ft from any house foundation, sidewalk, septic field or sewer line — the brittle limbs and aggressive surface roots make it a poor near-structure tree.[1][2] It is too large and thirsty for long-term container culture; grow it only in the open ground near a water source.[2]
care.sun: Full sun — six or more hours of direct light a day; it is shade-intolerant and grows fast and straight in the open.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable to almost any soil from coarse sand to clay, and tolerant of acid through alkaline ground (about pH 4.5–8.0), so Boulder's high-pH clay is no obstacle; it grows best on deep, moist fine sandy loams.[1][2]
care.water: This is a thirsty riparian tree — in the wild it grows on floodplains and ditchbanks where moisture, not soil type, governs it.[2] On the Front Range give it a moist, deep-watered site or a spot near a stream, pond or irrigation ditch; it is NOT a tree for a dry, xeric yard and will decline without ample water.[1][2]
care.spacing: Give it enormous room — a mature plains cottonwood reaches 60–100 ft tall and 35–60 ft wide with a broad open crown, so allow 40+ ft from buildings, drives and other large trees.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest of any tree from hardwood cuttings: take 8–20 in dormant cuttings of pencil-thick first-year wood in late winter and stick them in moist ground — they root readily, the basis of riparian-restoration plantings.[2][3] From seed, the cottony seed is viable only 1–2 weeks and needs no stratification: sow fresh onto bare, constantly moist mineral soil in full sun; collect catkins just before they open and dry, then rub out the seed and refrigerate (~41°F) briefly if not sowing at once.[2][3]
care.maintenance: Rake up the abundant leaf and twig litter it sheds through the season.[1][3]
care.troubles: Brittle, fast-grown wood means storm and ice breakage is the chief problem — the trade-off for its speed.[3] Cytospora canker, dieback and leaf-spot/rust diseases occur, and borers, aphids, scale and caterpillars feed on it; aphid honeydew can drip from the canopy.[1] The shallow, greedy roots lift sidewalks, invade sewer and drain lines and compete hard with lawn and garden — keep it well away from structures and paving.[1][2]
care.companions: Pair it with other native riparian/bottomland plants that share its love of moisture: mountain alder, red-twig dogwood, Wood's rose, golden currant (*Ribes aureum*), chokecherry, and a sedge/native-grass understory — a streamside community rather than a dry-garden bed.[2]
care.pruning: Prune young cottonwoods deliberately for structure — this is a fast, brittle-wooded tree that sheds limbs in wind and ice, so build one strong central leader early and remove weak, narrow-angled, and crossing branches while they are still small.[3] Do the work in full dormancy or in late summer, avoiding the heavy spring sap flow, cutting back to the branch collar, and keep deadwood cleared out as the tree develops.[1][3] Even a well-pruned cottonwood will drop some limbs in storms — that is the price of its speed — so never let large, heavy branches overextend over a house, drive, or walk.[3]

edible.card: Inner bark & spring catkins (survival food, cooked)
edible.summary: A documented Indigenous and survival food in its parts: the spring inner bark/cambium and the young catkins were eaten — but it is a salicin-bearing tree, so people sensitive to aspirin should avoid it, and it is a famine food, not a salad green.[1][3]
edible.parts: The sweet inner bark (cambium layer) was scraped and eaten in spring — the Teton Dakota and other Plains peoples used cottonwood inner bark as food — and the immature male catkins were eaten or, with the sticky sap, chewed like gum.[1][3] These are lean survival/famine foods, not a regular vegetable.[3]
edible.preparation: Harvest the inner bark only in spring when the sap is running and it peels cleanly; eat it raw in strips or dry and grind it as a flour extender, and cook the young catkins before eating.[3] Take bark sparingly and never girdle a tree — stripping the cambium all the way around kills it.[1]
edible.caution: Cottonwood bark, buds and leaves contain salicin (a salicylate, the aspirin precursor), so anyone allergic or sensitive to aspirin, pregnant, or on blood-thinners should NOT eat it.[1][3] This is a survival food eaten in small amounts, not a culinary green — the leaves and resinous buds are bitter and not eaten as food. Be certain of identification (the deltoid leaves and flattened petioles), since this is foraged from large wild trees.[3]
